title: Cervical Radiculopathy -- Introduction
content: Cervical radiculopathy is a common neck disorder that reduces function, productivity, and quality of life. The natural course is generally favorable. However, significant pain, progressive neurologic loss, or myelopathic signs warrant escalation of care.

title: Cervical Radiculopathy -- Prognosis
content: A systematic review of randomized controlled trials has demonstrated that surgery results in faster reductions in pain and disability during the first year. After 12 months, the functional differences between surgical and nonsurgical care narrow. [28] A study modelled outcomes after anterior surgical techniques for cervical degenerative radiculopathy. Unsuccessful cases correlated with heavy work, low education, litigation, prior surgery, symptoms longer than 3 months, high baseline disability, and anxiety. Smoking and a foreign mother tongue further predicted persistent arm pain. [29] Postoperative arm pain and disability improve less in C8 radiculopathy compared with C5 to C7. Surgery can relieve arm pain more effectively than numbness, which can often persist. [30]

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Cervical Vertebrae -- Clinical Significance
content: Neck pain presents a significant socioeconomic burden. Studies estimate the prevalence of neck pain in the adult population to be between 10% and 30%, making it one of the leading causes of disability globally. Neck pain is often classified based on duration (acute, subacute, or chronic) and etiology (eg, mechanical, neuropathic, rheumatologic). Frequent neck pain is defined as having at least 3 neck pain episodes in a year. [3] [11]

title: Cervical Radiculopathy -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Neck pain is a prevalent issue that can cause significant pain and disability. Up to 40% of work absenteeism is due to individuals with a history of neck pain. Cervical radiculopathy occurs when a nerve root in the spine is compressed or impeded, leading to pain that can spread beyond the neck and into the arm, chest, shoulders, and upper back. Common signs of impingement include muscle weakness and impaired deep tendon reflexes. These deficits may worsen over time, resulting in a decline in quality of life and daily functioning. While most symptoms can be treated with supportive care, more severe signs of functional loss may require rehabilitation or surgery.

title: Cervical Spondylosis -- Epidemiology
content: Symptomatic cervical spondylosis most commonly presents as neck pain. In the general population, the point prevalence of neck pain ranges from 0.4% to 41.5%, the 1-year incidence ranges from 4.8% to 79.5%, and lifetime prevalence may be as high as 86.8%. According to the Global Burden of Disease 2015, low back and neck pain remain the leading cause of years lived with disability and the fourth leading cause of disability-adjusted life years. [3] [4] [9]

title: Cervical Spondylosis -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Cervical spondylosis, the most prevalent degenerative condition of the cervical spine, predominantly affects individuals aged 60 and older. Progressive degenerative changes, including intervertebral disc desiccation, reduced disc height, spinal alignment alterations, and degeneration of uncovertebral and facet joints, characterize this condition. Osteophyte formation frequently accompanies these changes, potentially causing axial neck pain, cervical radiculopathy, or cervical myelopathy, significantly impairing daily function and quality of life. As a natural aging process, spondylosis affects most individuals after the fifth decade, with the cervical spine’s unique role in supporting extensive motion and protecting neural and vascular structures increasing its susceptibility to degeneration. Symptoms manifest as neck pain and stiffness, often with radicular symptoms when neural structures are compressed. Given its prevalence and associated disability, cervical spondylosis imposes a substantial economic burden, necessitating timely recognition and evidence-based management to mitigate this condition's impact on patients’ lives.

title: Cervical Spondylosis -- Prognosis
content: Cervical spondylosis is a slowly progressive, degenerative disease process that deteriorates with age. However, the severity of symptoms may not necessarily correlate with the degree of spondylosis seen on neuroimaging. Patients who present with axial neck pain typically improve over time, but can have a recurrence of pain. One study's results noted that 79% of patients with neck pain improved or became asymptomatic by the 15-year follow-up after the onset of symptoms. [47] Anywhere from 50% to 75% of persons with current neck pain report neck pain again 1 to 5 years later.

title: Cervical Myelopathy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Shared decision-making underpins ethical, patient-centered care. By discussing the natural history of untreated myelopathy and individualized prognostic factors (such as preoperative functional status and symptom duration), clinicians help patients weigh the benefits and risks of surgical intervention. Regular multidisciplinary case reviews and quality-metric tracking (encompassing time to decompression, complication rates, and patient-reported outcomes) enable continuous refinement of clinical pathways. Through clear communication, defined roles, and iterative process improvement, the healthcare team can enhance functional recovery and minimize long-term disability in patients with cervical myelopathy.

title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Cervical Spine -- Clinical Significance
content: Neck pain is a common disability, particularly in older people. The annual prevalence rate of neck pain is more than 30%. It can be classified according to etiology (mechanical, neuropathic, or referred) or duration (acute, subacute, or chronic.). Many rheumatologic conditions are also associated with neck pain, such as chronic rheumatoid arthritis. [3] [23] Any cause of neck pain should be evaluated and treated promptly. Otherwise, it may be followed by long-term disabilities. Proper history, examination, and investigations can differentiate neuropathic pain from mechanical neck pain. Initially, an X-ray may be used to visualize any dislocation or fractures. Computed tomography (CT) has superiority over X-ray. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is used to better view any soft tissue (spinal cord or ligament) injury. [23] [24]

title: Cervical Sprain -- Prognosis
content: Prognosis varies secondary to comorbidities prior to the injury, severity of WAD, age and socioeconomic environment.  Full recovery has been shown to occur in a few days to several weeks. [11] However, disability can be permanent and range from chronic pain to impaired physical function. [11] There have been inadequate studies that incorporate mitigating factors, such as socioeconomic and legal, which can impact an accurate assessment of recovery.  In regions where there is little or no litigation, the prognosis is more favorable lending that economic gain for disability may play a role in determining the patient's reports of full recovery. content: Cervical discogenic pain syndrome is a common source of neck pain with a reported prevalence between 16% to 41%. title: Cervical Discogenic Syndrome(Archived) -- Introduction
content: Chronic neck pain is a considerable public health burden that accounts for one of the top five chronic pain conditions. [1] The pain can range from cervical intervertebral discs, facet joints to atlantoaxial joints. Cervical discogenic pain syndrome (CDPS) is a common source of neck pain with a reported prevalence between 16% to 41%. [2] Cervical discs have a rich supply of nerve fibers that are prone to structural disruption and inflammatory reaction that makes them susceptible to pain. The commonly affected levels are C5/C6 and C6/C7, with C7 being the most common nerve root involved. [3] Initially, the symptoms present proximally, but they can later progress to brachialgia. The pain can be resolved with conservative therapy, but refractory pain may require further intervention.

content: Low back pain is the second most significant cause of absenteeism in the workplace, behind upper respiratory tract infections. title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Inter-scalene Triangle -- Clinical Significance -- Interscalene Block
content: After surgery in the postoperative period, nerve blocks may be used to decrease the pain experienced by the patient (see Image. Interscalene Block). One widely used example of such a nerve block is the inter-scalene nerve block, used specifically in many upper extremity injuries. [16] This nerve block can be used before or after shoulder surgery, allowing anesthesia of the suprascapular, lateral pectoral, musculocutaneous, axillary nerves, and parts of the cervical plexus. [17] The needle is inserted in a mediolateral fashion to reduce the risk of damage to important associated nearby nervous structures, including the long thoracic and dorsal scapular nerves. [2] The risk of long-term complications following interscalene nerve blocks was found in 1 prospective trial to be 0.4%; however, given the critical nature of the upper limb to daily life, any dysfunction is highly impactful on a patient's quality of life (see Image. Interscalene Block). [18] [19] [20]

Within the procedural taxonomy, Excision serves as a parent category for “Clinical actions A – G,” indicating that these clinical actions are sub‑categories of the broader excision concept. At the same time, certain spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision grouping; these include laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina (without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy) for one or two vertebral segments in the cervical, thoracic, or sacral regions, as well as multisegment laminectomy (> 2 vertebral segments) in the cervical and thoracic regions. This delineation clarifies that such laminectomy techniques are not considered forms of Excision. Excision is also linked to procedures involving the head and neck region. title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2023
content: Various late effects of bone injuries are directly linked to the underlying fracture. The “Late effect of fracture of multiple bones,” the “Late gevolgen van fractuur van schedel en aangezichtsbeenderen” (late consequences of fracture of the skull and facial bones), the Arabic phrases “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف السفلية” (late effects of fracture of the lower limbs), “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر الأطراف العلوية” (late effects of fracture of the upper limbs), and “الآثار المتأخرة لكسر عنق فخذ” (late effects of fracture of the femoral neck) are all associated with “كسر,” the Arabic term for fracture. This relationship underscores that regardless of the anatomical region—whether multiple bones, the skull and facial skeleton, the upper or lower extremities, or the femoral neck—post‑traumatic sequelae are fundamentally connected to the original fracture event, highlighting the importance of monitoring and managing these late complications in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
content: Cervical os patulous is synonymous with قصور عنق الرحم, a condition characterized by cervical insufficiency. Individuals classified as high risk are clinically associated with قصور عنق الرحم, indicating that patients with this risk profile are more likely to exhibit or develop cervical os patulous and its related complications. Recognizing the equivalence between cervical os patulous and قصور عنق الرحم, as well as its link to high‑risk status, is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in obstetric care.
